speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're exploring accessibility agents: what they are, where they run, how to decide whether you're actually ready to use one, and how to contribute to an open ecosystem of agents without treating automation like magic.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I'm glad we're starting there, because a lot of learners hear "AI agents" and think, great, now the tool will just do accessibility for me.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right, and this chapter pushes back on that. The core idea is simple: skill first, agent second. An agent multiplies a skill you already have. It does not create that skill for you.

speaker: ALEX
text: A quick orientation note before we go deeper. There's an audio companion for this material, related appendices for agents and Copilot reference material, and a set of authoritative sources from GitHub Docs and VS Code Docs. If you want the official product behavior, those docs are the source of truth.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this lesson is teaching practice and workflow, but not replacing the official documentation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Also, there's an important product note: newer VS Code versions include an Agents window in stable as a preview feature. That does not replace custom accessibility agents. It gives you a dedicated place to run and organize agent sessions, review changed files, and manage work across projects.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So learners should think, custom agents are still the substance, and the Agents window is one interface for working with them.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfectly said.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's frame the landscape. The repository highlighted in this chapter is a growing open source ecosystem of AI-powered agents organized into three teams and deployable across five platforms.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I noticed one number early on says fifty-five agents, and another later says eighty. How should learners hear that?

speaker: ALEX
text: As a living project. The exact count can change as the ecosystem grows. The important ideas are the structure and the breadth: three teams, multiple platforms, and a wide range of workflows. You are not expected to use every agent. You're expected to find the agents that match skills you already built manually.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the platforms matter too, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. These agents aren't trapped in one editor. The chapter positions them across GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Claude Desktop, and Codex CLI. So the lesson isn't "use this one exact interface." It's "understand the pattern, then choose the platform that fits your workflow and access needs."

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before anyone starts, what's the checklist?

speaker: ALEX
text: There's a real prerequisites checklist, and it matters. First, learners should already have workshop setup completed: Git, VS Code, and a GitHub account. Second, GitHub Copilot should already be enabled and working in VS Code. Third, Copilot access is required, but the free tier is enough for this workshop. Fourth, your repository should include a.github slash agents folder, or you should be prepared to create custom agents.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then there's the deeper prerequisite: not just software, but skill.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The chapter explicitly maps agent categories to manual experience. For GitHub workflow agents like daily briefings, issue tracking, pull request review, and analytics, you should already know how to navigate repositories, file issues, review pull requests, and explore repository insights.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I've never reviewed a pull request by hand, I should not ask an agent to review one and assume I understand the result.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. For accessibility monitoring agents, you should already know how to file an accessibility bug, apply accessibility labels, and inspect things like heading hierarchy. For template creation agents, you should already have designed and tested templates manually. For web accessibility auditing agents, you should already understand WCAG success criteria and have tested pages with assistive technology.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the document-specific agents have their own manual prerequisites too.

speaker: ALEX
text: They do. If you want help with Word, Excel, PDF, or EPUB accessibility, you should already know something about document structure and manual accessibility review. If you want remediation agents, you should understand tagging, alternative text, bookmarks, or the relevant toolchain. Python or wxPython agents assume Python knowledge and GUI accessibility basics. Developer tool agents assume CI or testing experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the self-check question is: could I do this manually right now?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. If the answer is no, learn the manual skill first.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This chapter also includes a workshop recommendation and a challenge set, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. This is an exploration and validation chapter. The challenge sequence asks learners to discover agents, validate one against their own manual understanding, inspect an agent's instructions, and optionally improve an existing agent or propose a new one.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's walk that in plain language.

speaker: ALEX
text: Challenge 15.1 is agent discovery mapping. The goal is to identify three to five agents that match skills you already have from earlier workshop work. The recommended starting set, especially if the ecosystem feels large, is daily briefing, issue tracker, and pull request review.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the workflow is practical.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very practical. Fork the accessibility-agents repository on GitHub. Open the repository. Review the list of agents. For each one, ask yourself whether you've performed that task manually. Then post a comment on your assigned challenge issue that maps each chosen agent to the manual skill that makes you ready.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So not just "I want to use this," but "I am ready because I already did this work myself."

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then comes validation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Challenge 15.2 asks you to clone your fork into VS Code or open it in github.dev, open Copilot Chat, choose one agent from your discovery list, run a simple prompt, and then evaluate the output against your manual expectations.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That evaluation step feels important.

speaker: ALEX
text: It's essential. The automation check for this chapter is explicitly none. Agent output always requires human judgment. You're expected to read the output carefully and say whether it looks accurate, helpful, incomplete, or questionable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And Challenge 15.3 goes inside the agent itself.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Agent instructions deep dive. You read one agent source file and assess what it can and cannot do. That helps learners move from "AI said a thing" to "I understand how this tool is framed, constrained, and prompted."

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there are optional hackathon-style extensions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Improve an existing agent by finding a gap in its instructions and opening a pull request, or propose a new agent for a workflow the ecosystem doesn't yet cover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And when learners finish?

speaker: ALEX
text: They submit evidence. The expected evidence is usually an issue comment showing agent output and your evaluation. The expected outcomes are that you can match agents to your current skills, test them critically, inspect their instructions, and begin thinking like a contributor rather than just a user.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if someone gets stuck?

speaker: ALEX
text: The chapter explicitly normalizes that. If you get stuck, narrow the scope. Start with the simplest agents. Use one short prompt. Compare results to something you already know how to do manually. The learning moment is not "the agent was perfect." The learning moment is "I can judge what the agent did."

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's even a teaching pattern behind this.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes: explore, validate, read internals, optionally contribute. That's the learning pattern of the chapter.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also a capstone note that sounds unusually direct.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does, and we should keep it. One of the most important tasks in the chapter is to share workshop feedback. Not busywork--feedback that helps improve the curriculum, tooling, and accessibility of the learning experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So submitting workshop feedback is framed as a genuine contribution.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I saw a table of contents in the source, but for listeners, maybe we should turn that into a road map.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good idea. Here's the road map in plain speech: first, the guiding principle of skill first, agent second. Then setup and configuration. Then the ecosystem and team structure. Then multi-platform deployment beyond VS Code. Then detailed hands-on reference for specific agents. Then slash commands and prompts. Then contributing your own agents or improving existing ones. Then the bigger orchestration picture. Then GitHub Desktop, GitHub CLI, and Copilot CLI. And finally, accessibility shortcuts, troubleshooting, and the official source trail.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's come back to the principle, because it is the foundation for everything else. Every agent has a manual prerequisite. If you have not done the corresponding skill by hand, the agent is not ready for you yet, and you are not ready for it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds strict, but honestly, it's protective.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very much so. Without that rule, agents create false confidence. A learner might get a polished summary of issues without understanding labels, timelines, duplicate reports, or severity. Or get a PR review without understanding diffs, testing, or risk.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the "learning cards" for this principle would be things like: automation amplifies skill, manual fluency comes first, and trust requires verification.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Those are the takeaways.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now setup. If someone wants to begin using these agents, what does that look like?

speaker: ALEX
text: The chapter includes a quick install path with platform-specific one-command setup for macOS or Linux and for Windows PowerShell. The exact commands belong in the written material because they can change, but the idea is that the repository is designed to be installed and explored quickly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So in audio, we emphasize the workflow, not recite volatile commands.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Then comes workshop setup: fork the repository, clone your fork locally, and open it in VS Code or another supported environment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's a prerequisites section inside setup too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It reinforces the need for Git, GitHub, VS Code, and Copilot readiness. Then there's a key concept: how agents are discovered.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Meaning how the platform even knows an agent exists.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. Agents typically travel with the repository through convention-based files and folders, especially under.github. That means repository context matters. If the repo includes agent definitions, compatible tools can detect and expose them.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That "travel with your repo" idea is powerful. It means the workflow can move with forks and collaborators.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And there's also an optional personalization step. You can customize preferences like your GitHub username, the repositories you work on most, your preferred output format, and notification priority.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this chapter even includes a personal preferences section.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. In effect, it models the kinds of settings that make agent output more useful and more accessible to you. Maybe you want concise bullet points. Maybe you prefer structured sections. Maybe you want urgent notifications first. Those preferences shape the interaction.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's talk about invoking agents on GitHub dot com, because not everyone wants to live in the editor.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. There are two main web-based paths highlighted. One is Copilot Chat with task mode. The other is assigning an issue to Copilot.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So learners can interact in the browser, not just locally.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and the chapter also points out browser-native Copilot features on GitHub itself: pull request summaries, pull request review help, Copilot in issues, GitHub Models as a free AI playground, and Copilot-drafted release notes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds broader than "custom agents."

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. The lesson is partly about custom agents and partly about the surrounding AI ecosystem on GitHub. The learning cards for this section would emphasize setup, discovery, repo-scoped behavior, and browser-native options.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now the ecosystem section. We mentioned three teams earlier. Let's unpack them.

speaker: ALEX
text: Team one is Accessibility, the largest cluster. These agents focus on accessibility workflows: auditing, checking, remediating, and tracking accessibility concerns across web, documents, and code-adjacent tasks.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Team two is GitHub Workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Those agents support issue triage, PR review, daily status awareness, analytics, and workflow management around collaboration.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And team three is Developer Tools.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. That group covers more technical workflows like CI accessibility checks, test automation, and tool-assisted development patterns.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there's a supporting ecosystem beyond the agents themselves.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The chapter highlights that the ecosystem includes not only agent files but prompts, slash commands, cloud features, hooks, platform integrations, and contribution pathways. For example, hook-based enforcement in tools like Claude Code can apply checks or automation rules at the right moment in a development workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So an agent isn't always a chat personality. Sometimes it's part of a workflow system.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said. The chapter also asks a design question: what would you build? That opens the contribution paths.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And those paths include improving an existing agent, proposing a new one, adding new workflows, and helping shape the roadmap.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The roadmap section reminds learners this is evolving. You're not studying a finished museum exhibit. You're entering a live open source ecosystem.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then we go beyond VS Code entirely.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Multi-platform deployment is a major idea here. The same general agent concepts can work across five platforms, but each platform has different strengths.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's do the "when to use each" version.

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub Copilot is recommended for most learners because it integrates well with common GitHub and VS Code workflows. Claude Code is positioned for faster iteration, especially if you like command-line-centered work. Gemini CLI offers a free-access path that may be attractive to some learners. Claude Desktop can be useful for longer, conversational sessions. And Codex CLI belongs in the broader multi-platform picture for people who prefer that toolchain.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the platform comparison is really about capability, comfort, and access.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The chapter encourages choosing a primary platform, while also understanding that you may run the same agent across multiple platforms depending on the task.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you give an example?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. You might discover and discuss an issue in GitHub's web interface, investigate locally in VS Code, and then use a CLI-based workflow for fast iteration or scripting. Same conceptual agent, different interaction layer.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the chapter includes learning paths by platform.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. Path A is for people who use VS Code and is the recommended path for most learners. Path B is for people who prefer the command line. Path C is for learners who want the best of both.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also explicit accessibility guidance by platform, which I'm glad to see.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very important. The chapter addresses screen reader, low vision, and keyboard navigation considerations across platforms. That means learners should actively evaluate not just what a platform can do, but how accessible it is for them in practice.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So "best platform" is personal, not universal.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Next steps in this section are: choose your primary platform, run a simple agent there, and compare whether another platform gives you a better accessible experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now for the hands-on reference section. This is where specific agents are introduced.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and the chapter first distinguishes two types of agents: informational agents and task-oriented agents.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Informational means read-only, analysis, summaries, status reports.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Task-oriented means agents that may edit files, run commands, or take more direct action, depending on the platform and permissions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's go one by one through the featured examples.

speaker: ALEX
text: Agent one is daily briefing. Its job is to produce a morning overview of what needs your attention.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What kinds of things show up in that briefing?

speaker: ALEX
text: The chapter gives a structured output format that may include issues opened in the last twenty-four hours, pull requests awaiting your review, CI failures on your branches, security alerts, and community activity.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it's a prioritization assistant.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Example commands are short, like asking for a morning briefing. The output is organized so you can scan it quickly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Agent two is issue tracker.

speaker: ALEX
text: That agent helps with issue management. It can find open issues, especially by label or other criteria, and support triage thinking.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The example output includes open issues labeled good-first-issue, broken into high and medium priority, plus a drafted reply for a particular issue.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and that's a good teaching moment: a drafted reply is not a final reply. It's a starting point that you must judge, revise, and own.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Agent three is pull request review.

speaker: ALEX
text: A very useful one, if you already know how to review manually. Its output may include a summary, a risk assessment, what could break, high-impact areas, a change map for specific files, suggested inline comments, and a recommendation such as approve with minor changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds impressive--and dangerous if someone trusts it blindly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A confident review summary can be wrong. The agent is useful because it helps you notice patterns and focus your attention. It does not replace your responsibility to read the diff and understand the impact.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Agent four is analytics.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Team analytics looks at contribution summaries, active contributors, high-churn files, review turnaround time, and trends. This can help maintainers understand workflow patterns, but again, interpretation matters.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Agent five is insiders accessibility tracker.

speaker: ALEX
text: That one monitors accessibility-related changes. The example output includes recent accessibility changes, issues found, specific problems like a heading hierarchy skip or non-descriptive link text, positive changes such as an added ARIA label, and recommendations.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I noticed the example even references a VS Code setup heading inside that output.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, which reinforces that these agents can point at concrete artifacts in docs or code. But you still verify the underlying file and context yourself.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And agent six is template builder.

speaker: ALEX
text: An interactive issue template wizard. Instead of asking you to manually remember every field type and YAML detail, it can guide template creation. But once again, it's most useful if you have already built and tested templates manually, because then you can tell whether the generated structure actually fits your project.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there's a section on slash commands and prompts. Why isolate that as its own topic?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because prompt ergonomics matter. A good agent can still be hard to use if you don't understand how to invoke it effectively. The chapter highlights the most useful slash commands for the workshop and gives a quick reference.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And it teaches how to read slash command definitions, not just memorize command names.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. That's crucial. If you read a slash command definition carefully, you can usually infer the intended behavior, scope, and output style. The example slash command in the chapter is an accessibility update command, and the important question is not merely "what do I type," but "what behavior should I expect when I type it?"

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learning cards here would be: read the definition, understand the behavior, use short targeted prompts, and verify the output.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now my favorite part: contributing to the ecosystem.

speaker: ALEX
text: This chapter is very strong on that. It explains two types of custom agents and then shows the general file structure of an agent.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's list that structure in human terms.

speaker: ALEX
text: An agent definition typically includes its purpose, its capabilities, its domain knowledge, its responsibilities, and response guidelines. There's also frontmatter--metadata that describes what kind of agent it is and how it should behave.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the distinction between informational and task-oriented shows up again in the metadata.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Informational agents are read-only and limited to GitHub tools or similar lookup behavior. Task-oriented agents may edit files, execute shell commands, or use APIs, depending on environment permissions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter gives an informational example first.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, the insiders accessibility tracker. That shows a read-only monitoring pattern.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then a task-oriented example: the Markdown Accessibility Assistant.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That example introduces a key pattern called tiered decision-making.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Meaning the agent doesn't immediately charge ahead with edits.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. It can reason in steps: assess, identify issues, suggest or preview changes, and only then act more directly when appropriate. That pattern is especially important for accessibility work, where blind or low-vision contributors need output that is explainable, structured, and reviewable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter also lists required prerequisites for that assistant.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Again, you need the underlying accessibility and markdown knowledge first. Otherwise the automation can obscure the reasoning you should be learning.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you invoke custom agents?

speaker: ALEX
text: Several ways: from VS Code chat, from the VS Code Agents window, from GitHub dot com task mode, and from issue assignment flows. There's also environment setup guidance for cloud agents so they can operate in GitHub-hosted contexts.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone wants to create their own?

speaker: ALEX
text: The chapter walks them toward that. Start with the template builder agent as an exercise. First generate a template with the agent. Then extend the agent for your own project. Then do iterative refinement: compare what the agent produced, identify gaps, adjust instructions, and test again.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a very open-source way to learn.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. You're not just consuming AI output; you're shaping the tool.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There are also two pre-built workflows highlighted.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A guided accessibility template workflow and a guided security vulnerability template workflow. Each shows an invocation pattern, a workflow sequence, and in the security case an output shape.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the exercises ask learners to notice what they're adding and then continue refining.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The "what comes next" message is that custom agents are not static. You evolve them as project needs become clearer.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then the chapter zooms out again to the bigger picture: teams, orchestration, and work beyond the editor.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The three teams connect because real workflows connect. Accessibility work touches repository workflow. Repository workflow touches developer tooling. Orchestrator agents help coordinate across those boundaries.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So instead of one specialized agent doing everything, you may have multiple agents with narrower jobs that are composed together.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The chapter also calls out high-impact agents to try first and the slash commands that save the most time.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And it introduces scope at three levels.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Scope one is your editor: accessibility agents helping you locally while you work. Scope two is your repository: agent definitions traveling with forks and collaborators. Scope three is the cloud: GitHub-hosted agentic workflows that operate through issues, pull requests, task mode, or other cloud contexts.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That helps learners understand where the action is happening.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and it leads into deeper examples like auto-triaging accessibility issues. That kind of workflow can be powerful, but only if maintainers have clear criteria and review habits.

speaker: JAMIE
text: We also need to cover GitHub Desktop, GitHub CLI, and Copilot CLI.

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. GitHub Desktop appears as a simpler graphical path for repository operations. Then GitHub CLI, the gh tool, is covered with installation, authentication, and common commands.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Again, we won't recite every command in audio, but we should explain the role.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. GitHub CLI lets you inspect and manipulate GitHub resources from the terminal: repositories, issues, pull requests, authentication, and more. Then GitHub Copilot CLI extends that experience with AI assistance in the shell.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the example tasks are very concrete: explain a command, suggest a command, or write a shell script.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Those examples show where Copilot CLI can reduce friction. If you know what you want but not the syntax, it can help. If you have a command and want it explained in accessible terms, it can help. If you need a short script scaffold, it can help.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But the same caution applies.

speaker: ALEX
text: Always. Never paste or run shell commands you do not understand, especially commands that modify files, history, permissions, or remote state.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The example session in the chapter likely exists to show that rhythm: ask, inspect, understand, then decide.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And the learning cards for this section would emphasize that GitHub Desktop, GitHub CLI, and Copilot CLI are complementary tools, not competing identities.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also a quick-reference keyboard shortcut section.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For this audience, that matters a lot. The chapter includes accessibility-agents quick-reference shortcuts, including opening Copilot Chat and navigating key workflows. Since shortcuts vary by keymap and platform, learners should verify them in their own environment. The important practice is to know both the shortcut path and the Command Palette path.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That way if a shortcut conflicts or isn't memorable, you still have a reliable route.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's finish with troubleshooting, because agents never fail at convenient times.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very true. The chapter includes several common failure modes. First: "agent not found."

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should learners check?

speaker: ALEX
text: Check whether the agent definition exists in the expected repository location, whether the platform supports that style of custom agent, whether the repository is open in the correct context, and whether you may need to reload or rescan the environment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Second: the agent produces incorrect output.

speaker: ALEX
text: Then treat it as an analysis artifact, not truth. Compare it against the underlying issue, PR, file, or repository state. Tighten the prompt. Check whether the agent instructions are too vague. And if the agent itself needs improvement, that can become a contribution.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Third: slash command doesn't work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Check whether the command is supported in your current tool, whether the syntax matches the definition, and whether the command belongs to a different platform or agent context than the one you're using.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So troubleshooting here is partly technical and partly conceptual.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Sometimes the fix is "reload the window." Sometimes the fix is "I misunderstood what this tool can do."

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we close, can we pull together the authoritative-source idea one more time?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. This chapter repeatedly points back to GitHub Docs, GitHub Changelog, GitHub Copilot docs, custom instructions support matrices, and documentation about custom agents. That matters because AI features move quickly. If a menu label changes, if a support matrix expands, if the Agents window behavior shifts, the official docs outrank any static lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the durable learning here is not memorizing one interface screenshot.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The durable learning is: understand skill first, agent second; know how agent files travel with repositories; choose a platform that works for your workflow and accessibility needs; validate outputs against manual knowledge; read agent instructions; and contribute improvements when you find gaps.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let me see if I can summarize the learner mindset. Don't ask, "Which agent is coolest?" Ask, "Which agent matches a skill I already have?" Don't ask, "Can I trust the output?" Ask, "How do I verify the output?" And don't ask, "What if the agent is imperfect?" Ask, "Can I improve it?"

speaker: ALEX
text: That is exactly the mindset this chapter wants.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for the workshop tasks: map three to five agents to skills you already have, run one and evaluate it, read one agent's instructions closely, optionally contribute a fix or propose a new agent, and don't skip the feedback submission.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfect. Accessibility agents are most powerful when they stay in partnership with human judgment, human skill, and accessible contribution workflows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which is a very fitting note for this course.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. We'll stop there.
